questions.question: Why can't you just flatten an image's pixels into a transformer sequence?
options: Pixels are not numbers
A 224x224 RGB image becomes 150,528 tokens, and self-attention is quadratic in sequence length — a non-starter; patches compress the grid into a manageable sequence
Transformers only accept text
Flattening loses the color channels
questions.explanation: ViT's answer: carve the image into 16x16 patches, linearly project each, add positions — 196 tokens instead of 150k. Five years later every frontier model still begins this way.

questions.question: For ViT-B/16 at resolution 224, what is the token arithmetic?
options: 224 tokens, one per row
A 14x14 grid of 16x16 patches → 196 patch tokens, each a 768-value pixel cube projected to D=768, plus a [CLS] token → 197
1,000 tokens, one per class
3 tokens, one per channel
questions.explanation: And the "linear projection" is implemented as nn.Conv2d(3, D, kernel_size=P, stride=P) — mathematically identical, computationally efficient.

questions.question: Why did 2D-RoPE replace learnable 1D position tables in modern vision backbones?
options: It uses less memory only

Learnable tables tie the model to the training resolution (interpolation needed at new grid sizes); 2D-RoPE rotates Q/K by (row, column) index, so arbitrary grids work at inference with no position table
1D positions were patented
RoPE is required by SigLIP's license
questions.explanation: Relative 2D position emerges from rotation angles. This is what enables native-resolution and any-aspect-ratio processing in Qwen2-VL's M-RoPE and SigLIP 2.

questions.question: What problem do register tokens solve?
options: They store the image's EXIF metadata
ViTs without a sink token develop high-norm artifact patches that hijack self-attention — 4-16 learnable registers absorb that load, improving dense-prediction quality
They register the model with the hub
They cache attention for speed
questions.explanation: Darcet et al. 2023's observation; DINOv2 and SigLIP 2 both ship registers. They're scaffolding, not content — discarded before patch tokens are handed to an LLM.

questions.question: How does pretraining objective determine what a vision backbone is good for?
options: It doesn't — all backbones are interchangeable
CLIP/SigLIP (contrastive with text) for semantic matching; DINOv2 (self-distillation, no labels) for dense visual features; MAE (masked reconstruction) as a finetuning starting point
Only supervised ImageNet pretraining works
The objective only affects training speed
questions.explanation: The 2026 question isn't "patches or not" but which descendant: SigLIP 2 dominates open-VLM vision towers for text alignment, while DINOv2 is the default for segmentation/depth-style dense features.
